Anna's Farm

Common Space Entry Landscape

Hardscape Proposal

A site-specific geological sculpture inspired by the Flatirons of Boulder, Colorado — transforming two community entry areas into landmark landscape features using vertical Colorado red flagstone, native granite boulders, and dry creek bed composition.

Vision Site 1 Site 2 Estimate Terms
Design vision

Flatirons-Inspired Geological Sculpture

This project transforms two neglected common-space entry areas at Anna's Farm into distinctive community landmarks. The design draws directly from the Flatirons of Boulder — the iconic tilted sandstone slabs visible from across the Front Range — and translates that geological drama into a site-specific sculptural installation using native Colorado materials.

Signature feature: vertical red flagstone

Colorado red flagstone slabs are installed upright — not flat, not walkable — at varied heights and tilts, composed to create a mountain ridge silhouette. The top edges of each slab are profiled to evoke the Flatirons ridgeline. Smaller standing flagstone pieces (6–8" wide, 1–2 ft above grade) are arranged in semi-arcs throughout the planting bed, echoing the main formation at a smaller scale.

The Flatirons — Boulder, Colorado

Concept sketch — William Held

Material palette

Vertical elements — Colorado red flagstone slabs standing upright in Flatirons-style formation, plus Colorado red strip stone at base transitions. Smaller standing flagstone pieces in semi-arc groupings. All vertical elements have mountain range silhouettes on their top edges.

Ground plane — River rock and granite stones shaped as a dry creek bed flowing through and around the vertical formations. Contrasting decorative stone (2"–6" diameter) fills all surrounding ground areas outside the creek bed. Nothing else touches the ground — the vertical stone is the show.

Anchoring — Native Colorado granite and river boulders provide scale contrast and anchor the composition, grounding the vertical flagstone in a natural geological context.

Site one

Common Space Entry A

Anna's Farm HOA — Site 1  ·  40.000458°N, -105.110036°W  ·  Elevation 5,360 ft

3,500
Square feet
230
Ft perimeter
$21,050
Site subtotal

Existing conditions

Corner lot at Anna Thomas Parkway and Baseline Road. Currently bare compacted soil with deteriorated landscape fabric, patchy dead grass, and scattered debris. The Anna's Farm monument sign with horse logo on stone pillars anchors the corner. A mature deciduous tree and utility infrastructure remain in place — the design works around them.

Site 1 existing conditions — April 2026. Mockup renders will replace these photos.

Proposed installation

The larger of the two sites receives the more articulated Flatirons formation — multiple vertical red flagstone slab groupings creating a dramatic ridge silhouette. 4–6 standing flagstone pieces in a semi-arc echo the main formation. A flowing dry creek bed wraps through the composition, surrounded by contrasting decorative stone. Six tons of native Colorado granite boulders anchor the scene. Ten solar accent lights provide evening drama, uplighting the vertical stone faces.

Site two

Common Space Entry B

Anna's Farm HOA — Site 2  ·  40.001489°N, -105.109675°W  ·  Elevation 5,350 ft

1,500
Square feet
194
Ft perimeter
$12,450
Site subtotal

Existing conditions

Entry plot at Homestead Street. Currently overgrown with neglected ornamental trees, dead grass, a couple of existing boulders, and an antique farm implement. Split-rail fence along the back boundary with open prairie behind. Townhomes flanking the right side.

Site 2 existing conditions — Google Street View, April 2023. Mockup renders will replace these photos.

Proposed installation

A simplified version of the Site One concept — a single focal cluster of vertical red flagstone slabs forming a compact Flatirons ridge. 3–4 standing flagstone pieces in a semi-arc provide supporting vertical rhythm. Same dry creek bed and contrasting stone ground treatment. Four tons of native granite boulders. The split-rail fence backdrop and prairie context complement the Colorado geological narrative.

Terms & conditions

Project terms

Scope of work. All labor and materials as described in the line-item estimates above. Scope is limited to hardscape installation — no sod, planting, or irrigation is included. Work will be performed at the two designated common-space entry sites within the Anna's Farm subdivision.

Materials. Colorado red flagstone sourced from Colorado quarries. Native Colorado granite and river boulders. All stone is natural and will have inherent variation in color, texture, and profile — this variation is a feature of the design, not a defect.

Timeline. Estimated project duration to be confirmed upon scheduling. Weather-dependent. Start date contingent upon receipt of the 10% scheduling deposit.

Warranty. Workmanship warranty per contractor standard terms. Natural stone materials are not warranted against weathering, as natural aging enhances the geological character of the installation.

Acceptance. To proceed, please sign below and remit the 10% scheduling deposit of $3,350.
